### **Glossary of Terms**

A quick reference for key terminology used in this document.

*   **BFP (Burst Flag Pulse):** A timing pulse generated by the **VDC-NUS** chip (U4) that gates the color burst window on each scanline. It signals to the downstream encoder (**ENC-NUS**, U5) the precise interval during which the **colorburst** reference signal should be inserted into the back porch of the composite output. The burst gate window duration is approximately 5.1 μs per oscilloscope observation. *See also: Colorburst, CSYNC.*

*   **Colorburst (Subcarrier, fS):** A high-frequency sine wave added to the video signal that serves as the timing reference for encoding and decoding color information. Its frequency is precisely defined by the broadcast standard (**NTSC**, **PAL**, and **PAL-M**) and is the foundational constant from which nearly all other timing values are derived. *Symbols: **fS**, **f_colorburst**.*

*   **CSYNC (Composite Sync):** A signal generated by the **VDC-NUS** (U4) that combines horizontal sync (**HSYNC**) and vertical sync (**VSYNC**) into a single waveform. **CSYNC** is passed to the **ENC-NUS** encoder (U5) and embedded in the final composite video output, allowing a display to lock to the signal's horizontal and vertical timing simultaneously. *See also: VSYNC, BFP.*

*   **DSYNC (VDC_DSYNC):** A continuous timing signal transmitted from the **RCP** (U9) to the **VDC-NUS** (U4) on the digital video bus. When the signal is low, the accompanying data lines carry synchronization information rather than pixel color data. It goes low during Stage 0 of the four-stage **VDC bus** cycle to mark the start of a new pixel group. *See also: VDC Bus, VDC-NUS.*

*   **fH (Horizontal Scan Frequency):** The rate at which single horizontal lines of pixels are drawn on the screen, measured in Hertz (Hz). Also referred to as Line Frequency.

*   **fS:** Symbol for the color subcarrier frequency. Used interchangeably with **Colorburst** in derivations and signal relationships (e.g., **fS** = 227.5 × **fH** for **NTSC**). *See also: Colorburst, fH.*

*   **fV (Vertical Scan Frequency):** The rate at which the hardware completes a full vertical scan of the screen, from top to bottom. This is measured from one **VSYNC** pulse to the next and is colloquially known as **Refresh Rate**. In **progressive** scan modes, this is the *frame rate*; in **interlaced** modes, it is the *field rate*.

*   **Half-line (S):** The atomic unit of vertical timing used by the N64's **Video Interface (VI)**. A full scanline is composed of two half-lines. The total count of half-lines in a frame or field is a critical variable (**VI_V_TOTAL**) for calculating the **refresh rate**. *Symbol: **S**. See also: Terminal Count.*

*   **Interlaced (I):** A video scan method where a single frame is split into two separate fields: one containing all the odd-numbered lines, and one containing all the even-numbered lines. These fields are drawn sequentially, effectively doubling the perceived **refresh rate** at the cost of vertical resolution per refresh.

*   **L (VI Clocks per Line):** Symbol for the number of **VI** clock cycles that constitute one full horizontal scanline, as defined by the **VI_H_TOTAL** register. The effective value is **VI_H_TOTAL** + 1 (**terminal-count** convention). **L** is a primary integer divisor in the clock chain: f_line = f_vi / **L**. Values are 3,094 (**NTSC**), 3,178 (**PAL**), and 3,091 (**PAL-M**). *See also: Terminal Count, VI.*

*   **LEAP Register:** A hardware compensation mechanism used exclusively by **PAL** N64 consoles. It periodically adjusts the length of a scanline by one **VI** clock cycle to correct for the small fractional timing error that results from integer constraints in the horizontal timing registers. The adjustment follows a repeating 5-field B-A-B-A-B sequence, averaging 5.6 **VI** clocks per field, and maintains the exact 15,625 Hz line frequency required by the **PAL** timing standard. Not used in **NTSC** or **PAL-M** modes. *See also: PAL, f_vi.*

*   **Master Clock (f_xtal):** The primary, high-frequency crystal oscillator signal that drives the N64's **Reality Co-Processor (RCP)**. All video timing is derived from this single source through integer multiplication and division. On NUS-CPU-01 through NUS-CPU-07, **f_xtal** is produced by U7 (**MX8330MC**) from crystal X1. The **VI** clock (**f_vi**) is **f_xtal** multiplied by the region-specific factor M (17/5 for **NTSC** and **PAL-M**; 14/5 for **PAL**). ***f_xtal** is the sole true constant in all video timing derivations.*

*   **MX8330MC:** A single-channel Macronix clock synthesizer IC. In early N64 revisions (NUS-CPU-01 through NUS-CPU-07), two **MX8330MC** chips were used: U7 (**NTSC**/**PAL-M**, FSEL high → 17/5 multiplier) and U15 (**PAL**, FSEL low → 14/5 multiplier). Each chip outputs FSC (crystal ÷ 4, the color subcarrier reference) and FSO (the Rambus clock, from which FSO/5 drives the video domain). *See also: MX8350, Master Clock.*

*   **MX8350:** A dual-channel Macronix clock synthesizer that replaced the twin **MX8330MC** configuration in later N64 revisions (NUS-CPU-08 onward, 1999+). It consolidates both the **NTSC**/**PAL-M** and **PAL** clock synthesis functions into a single package with equivalent output frequencies. Timing values derived in this document are unaffected by this revision. *See also: MX8330MC.*

*   **NTSC:** National Television System Committee. The broadcast video standard used in North America, Japan, South Korea, and parts of Central America. Defines a 525-line, approximately 59.94 Hz **interlaced** signal. On the N64, the **NTSC** crystal is 315/22 MHz (exact), the **VI** clock multiplier is 17/5, and there are 3,094 **VI** clocks per line. *Not to be confused with PAL-M, which shares **NTSC**'s general line rate but is a distinct standard.*

*   **PAL:** Phase Alternating Line. The broadcast video standard used across Europe, Australia, New Zealand, and much of Africa and Asia. Defines a 625-line, 50 Hz **interlaced** signal with a **color subcarrier** of exactly 4,433,618.75 Hz. On the N64, the **PAL** crystal is 17.734475 MHz (exact), the **VI** clock multiplier is 14/5, and the **LEAP register** is required to maintain the exact 15,625 Hz line frequency. *Not to be confused with PAL-M.*

*   **PAL-M (MPAL):** A Brazilian broadcast standard that combines **PAL**-derived color encoding with an **NTSC**-derived line rate. It is a distinct standard – not a variant of **NTSC** or **PAL**. The **color subcarrier** is 511,312,500 / 143 Hz (containing a 127/143 fractional remainder), the crystal is 2,045,250,000 / 143 Hz, and there are 3,091 **VI** clocks per line. **PAL-M** does not use the **LEAP register**. Also referred to as MPAL in N64 source code and some documentation. Conflating **PAL-M** with either **NTSC** or **PAL** at any stage of a derivation produces incorrect results.

*   **Progressive (P):** A video scan method where all the lines of a frame are drawn sequentially from top to bottom in a single pass. This provides a more stable, solid image compared to **interlacing**.

*   **Raster:** The rectangular pattern of scan lines that an electron beam follows to draw an image on a CRT screen. The **raster** defines the total area of the signal, including both the visible picture and the blanking intervals.

*   **RCP (Reality Co-Processor):** The main Silicon Graphics co-processor in the N64 that handles both graphics (Reality Drawing Processor) and audio/system tasks (Reality Signal Processor). It contains the **Video Interface (VI)**, which generates the video timing signals.

*   **S (Half-Lines per Frame):** Symbol for the total **half-line** count per vertical scan cycle, as programmed via the **VI_V_TOTAL** register. The effective value is **VI_V_TOTAL** + 1 (**terminal-count** convention). **S / 2** gives the number of full scanlines. Values are 526 / 525 (**NTSC** and **PAL-M progressive** / **interlaced**) and 626 / 625 (**PAL**). *See also: Half-line, Terminal Count.*

*   **S-Video:** A two-channel analog video interface that carries luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) as separate signals, avoiding the quality loss of combining them into composite. On N64 hardware, **S-Video** output is generated natively by the DENC-NUS, AVDC-NUS, and MAV-NUS encoder variants. The **VDC-NUS** + **ENC-NUS** two-chip path found on NUS-CPU-01 through NUS-CPU-04 produces composite and RGB, but not **S-Video** directly.

*   **Terminal Count:** A register convention used by the N64's **Video Interface** in which the stored value is one less than the effective hardware count. To derive the actual number of clocks or **half-lines**, add 1 to the register value: effective **half-lines** = **VI_V_TOTAL** + 1; effective clocks per line = **VI_H_TOTAL** + 1. All timing derivations in this document apply this correction before any calculation.

*   **VDC Bus:** The digital video bus between the **RCP** (U9) and the **VDC-NUS** chip (U4). It carries seven bits of pixel data (**VDC_D0** through **VDC_D6**), the **DSYNC** timing signal, and a shared clock. Data is transmitted in a four-stage multiplexed cycle, one stage per **VI** clock: Stage 0 carries synchronization data with **DSYNC** held low; Stages 1-3 carry the Red, Green, and Blue color components of a single rendered pixel. One complete four-stage group constitutes one "**VI pixel**". *See also: DSYNC, VDC-NUS.*

*   **VDC-NUS / ENC-NUS / DENC-NUS / AVDC-NUS / MAV-NUS:** The family of video output chips used across N64 motherboard revisions, all performing the same logical function of converting the **RCP**'s digital video stream to an analog output signal. On NUS-CPU-01 through NUS-CPU-04, this is a two-chip path: **VDC-NUS** (BU9801F, U4) performs digital-to-analog conversion and generates **CSYNC** and **BFP**; **ENC-NUS** (U5) handles composite and RGB encoding, receiving the **color subcarrier** reference via its SCIN pin. Later revisions consolidate both functions into a single chip. This variation is not known to affect any timing values derived in this document.

*   **VI (Video Interface):** The hardware block within the **RCP** responsible for generating the N64's video signal. It reads from memory and uses a set of programmable registers (e.g., **VI_V_TOTAL**, **VI_H_TOTAL**) to define the timing, resolution, and format of the output signal.

*   **VSYNC (Vertical Synchronization):** A timing pulse in a video signal that indicates the end of a frame (or field) and instructs the display to return the electron beam to the top of the screen to begin the next scan. The frequency of these pulses defines the **refresh rate (fV)**.
